adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

adapters.name: flatfile
adapters.description: Every decision including rejected alternatives, dumped flat with no retrieval or ranking.

adapters.name: robrain
adapters.description: Top-5 active decisions by 5-signal composite score, rejected alternatives rendered as warnings.

reply.proposal: Implement a distributed session cache using Redis as the primary layer, with a local in-memory cache (LRU) on each API instance for frequently accessed sessions to reduce Redis round-trips. Sessions will be stored in Redis with TTL matching our session expiration policy, and we'll use cache-aside pattern with write-through for updates to maintain consistency. Each API instance will invalidate its local cache on session mutations via a pub/sub channel, ensuring eventual consistency across the fleet while dramatically reducing latency for hot sessions.
key_technologies: node-cache or similar local LRU cache library

reply.proposal: Deploy the webhooks service on Kubernetes using our existing EKS cluster, with CI/CD through GitHub Actions triggering container builds to ECR and automated deployments via ArgoCD. Configure service mesh (Istio) for traffic management and retry policies given the critical nature of webhook delivery. Set up PostgreSQL for webhook event persistence with read replicas for reliability, and implement Redis for request deduplication and rate limiting. Use Terraform to manage all infrastructure-as-code with separate staging and production environments, and configure CloudWatch for observability with PagerDuty alerting for delivery failures.

Implement a GraphQL layer as a public API gateway alongside or replacing the REST API, allowing clients to request only the specific fields they need. GraphQL's query language naturally eliminates overfetching by letting mobile clients specify exact field requirements, reducing payload sizes and bandwidth consumption. We can use Apollo Server to expose existing REST endpoints through a GraphQL schema, providing a gradual migration path without rewriting backend services. This approach also enables the mobile team to iterate independently on their data needs without coordinating API changes with backend teams.
key_technologies: GraphQL schema stitching or federation (for composing multiple backend services)
DataLoader (for batching and caching to prevent N+1 queries)

reply.proposal: Bundle the partner widget as a self-contained UMD module with all dependencies inlined, exposing a single initialization function that the host shell calls at runtime. Use a separate webpack/vite config that targets UMD output format, tree-shakes unused code, and generates a single .js file (plus optional .css) that can be loaded via <script> tag without requiring the host to manage the widget's dependency tree. This approach isolates the widget's build pipeline from the host shell while ensuring zero version conflicts and predictable runtime behavior.
key_technologies: webpack or vite with UMD target
CSS-in-JS or CSS extraction for style isolation

verdict.matchedOn: key_technologies: webpack or vite with UMD target

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- [2026-01-15] Cache session lookups in Redis — Standard choice for a low-latency session cache; sub-millisecond reads
- [2026-06-05] Move the session cache from Redis to a Postgres UNLOGGED table — UNLOGGED benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our read pattern, and drops a second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure
- [2026-01-22] Run the API on Kubernetes (EKS) — Industry-standard orchestration; room to grow into multi-service
- [2026-05-18] Run all backend services on ECS Fargate — Two engineers cannot carry cluster upgrades, CNI debugging and node pools; Fargate covers every workload we actually run
- [2026-02-12] Expose the public API as GraphQL — Clients pick their own fields; one endpoint for every screen
- [2026-06-20] Migrate the public API from GraphQL back to REST with sparse fieldsets — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation cost more than the flexibility bought us; ?fields= covers the overfetching complaint at a fraction of the complexity
- [2026-02-02] Bundle the dashboard with Vite — Sub-second cold starts and a simpler config than the alternatives
- [2026-06-15] Bundle the dashboard with Webpack, using Module Federation for embedded entry points — The partner-facing shell requires Module Federation at runtime; the Vite federation plugin was not production-ready when we needed it
- [2026-02-05] Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- [2026-03-10] All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- [2026-03-15] Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- [2026-05-12] OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- [2026-02-01] pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies
- [2026-04-25] User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer

reply.proposal: Implement an in-process memory cache with a write-through pattern to the Postgres UNLOGGED session table, using a consistent hashing scheme across ECS Fargate instances to route session lookups to the same container when possible. Add cache invalidation via a lightweight pub/sub mechanism (Redis Pub/Sub or SNS) so session mutations (logout, permission changes) broadcast invalidation across all instances. This avoids a second datastore while keeping hot sessions in memory at sub-millisecond latency, and aligns with our prior decision to consolidate on Postgres for session storage.
key_technologies: Node.js in-process LRU cache (lru-cache or node-cache)
Redis Pub/Sub or AWS SNS for cache invalidation broadcasts
Consistent hashing library (consistent-hash or similar) for instance affinity
OpenTelemetry instrumentation for cache hit/miss metrics
acknowledged_rejections: Returning to Redis as a dedicated session cache — we benchmarked and chose Postgres UNLOGGED to drop a second datastore
Distributed cache (Memcached) — adds operational overhead we rejected when moving off Redis
Database query caching at the ORM layer — we need explicit invalidation control across instances, not implicit TTL-based expiry

verdict.matchedOn: key_technologies: Redis Pub/Sub or AWS SNS for cache invalidation broadcasts

reply.proposal: Deploy the webhooks service on ECS Fargate, following our established pattern from the main API. Use the same pnpm workspace structure, Zod input validation at the boundary, structured pino logging, and OpenTelemetry tracing at 10% sample rate. Store webhook configurations and delivery state in Postgres (alongside session data in the UNLOGGED table), and use SQS for reliable webhook queuing to decouple ingestion from delivery. Expose health checks and metrics via a standard endpoint for ECS task monitoring.
acknowledged_rejections: Kubernetes/EKS — team decided Fargate eliminates cluster management overhead we cannot sustain
Redis for webhook queue — we consolidated to Postgres to reduce operational surface; SQS provides durability without a second datastore
GraphQL for webhook schema — we migrated back to REST with sparse fieldsets; webhooks follow the same pattern

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets on the existing REST API using the ?fields= query parameter, consistent with our June 2026 decision to migrate from GraphQL for exactly this use case. Each endpoint's Zod schema already defines available fields; we'll extend the schema validation to accept and enforce a fields parameter, filtering the response object before serialization. This gives mobile clients fine-grained control over payload size without the resolver complexity or cache invalidation overhead that made GraphQL uneconomical for us. Document the ?fields= syntax in OpenAPI via the Zod schema annotations, and add a middleware to strip undeclared fields from all responses.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — we migrated away in June 2026 due to resolver N+1 queries and cache invalidation complexity outweighing the flexibility benefit

reply.proposal: Bundle the partner widget as a Module Federation remote using Webpack, matching the existing shell architecture decided on 2026-06-15. The widget will expose its entry point as a federated module that the shell consumes at runtime, sharing dependencies (React, Zod schemas, pino for client-side logging) across the boundary to minimize bundle size. This reuses our proven Webpack + Module Federation setup rather than introducing a second bundler, keeps the widget's build pipeline consistent with the dashboard, and lets the shell control when and how the widget loads without a separate deployment or versioning concern.
acknowledged_rejections: Vite with federation plugin — not production-ready as of 2026-06-15 decision
Separate bundler (esbuild, Rollup) for the widget — adds build complexity and dependency duplication vs. reusing Webpack
Embedding the widget as a pre-built static bundle loaded from S3 — loses the runtime flexibility and shared dependency optimization that Module Federation provides

cells.context:
Project decision log:
- [2026-01-15] Cache session lookups in Redis — Standard choice for a low-latency session cache; sub-millisecond reads
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances
- [2026-06-05] Move the session cache from Redis to a Postgres UNLOGGED table — UNLOGGED benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our read pattern, and drops a second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure
  - REJECTED: staying on Redis — A second datastore's ops and on-call burden for a 15% read win we don't need
- [2026-01-22] Run the API on Kubernetes (EKS) — Industry-standard orchestration; room to grow into multi-service
  - REJECTED: plain EC2 with systemd — No rolling deploys or self-healing
- [2026-05-18] Run all backend services on ECS Fargate — Two engineers cannot carry cluster upgrades, CNI debugging and node pools; Fargate covers every workload we actually run
  - REJECTED: self-managed control plane — Operational surface far beyond what this team can staff
- [2026-02-12] Expose the public API as GraphQL — Clients pick their own fields; one endpoint for every screen
  - REJECTED: REST with fixed payloads — Mobile screens overfetch whole resources to read two fields
- [2026-06-20] Migrate the public API from GraphQL back to REST with sparse fieldsets — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation cost more than the flexibility bought us; ?fields= covers the overfetching complaint at a fraction of the complexity
  - REJECTED: staying on GraphQL — Resolver N+1s, no HTTP caching, and a schema only two people could safely change
- [2026-02-02] Bundle the dashboard with Vite — Sub-second cold starts and a simpler config than the alternatives
  - REJECTED: Parcel — Smaller plugin ecosystem for our needs
- [2026-06-15] Bundle the dashboard with Webpack, using Module Federation for embedded entry points — The partner-facing shell requires Module Federation at runtime; the Vite federation plugin was not production-ready when we needed it
  - REJECTED: Vite federation plugin — Experimental; broke shared-dependency versioning in the spike
- [2026-02-05] Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- [2026-03-10] All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- [2026-03-15] Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- [2026-05-12] OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- [2026-02-01] pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies
- [2026-04-25] User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer

reply.proposal: Implement an in-process memory cache with a write-through pattern to the Postgres UNLOGGED session table, using a pub/sub mechanism (via Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY or a lightweight message queue) to invalidate stale entries across ECS Fargate instances when sessions are updated. This gives us sub-millisecond reads on cache hits while keeping session state consistent across the fleet without introducing a second datastore beyond what we already operate. For cache misses, we fall back to the UNLOGGED table with its proven 15% overhead versus Redis—acceptable for the operational simplicity we've already committed to.
key_technologies: Node.js in-process LRU cache (lru-cache or similar)
acknowledged_rejections: in-process memory cache without invalidation — breaks consistency across multiple API instances
Redis — rejected in June 2026 decision; ops burden of a second datastore outweighs the 15% read latency win
staying with direct Postgres reads — the whole point is to reduce latency spikes on cache misses

reply.proposal: Deploy the webhooks service on ECS Fargate alongside the existing backend, using the same container image build and push pipeline we have for the API. Store webhook configurations and delivery state in Postgres (same database as the API, in a separate schema to isolate concerns), and use SQS for reliable webhook event queueing to decouple producers from the delivery worker. Instrument with pino structured logging and OpenTelemetry traces at 10% sample rate to match the API observability setup. Use the same Zod + problem+json error handling pattern for any webhook management endpoints.
key_technologies: CloudFormation or Terraform (IaC for SQS queue and IAM roles)
acknowledged_rejections: self-managed Kubernetes control plane — team cannot staff the operational burden
Redis for webhook event queue — we already chose Postgres over a second datastore for session caching
custom error shapes — we standardized on RFC 7807 problem+json
unstructured logging — we committed to pino JSON lines for ops dashboard compatibility

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets on the REST API using a ?fields= query parameter, validated and documented via Zod schemas at each endpoint. This gives mobile clients precise control over response payloads without the resolver complexity and cache-invalidation burden that GraphQL introduced. Each endpoint's Zod schema already serves as the OpenAPI source of truth; we extend it to mark fields as optional in the response, parse the ?fields= parameter against the schema, and return only requested fields. This approach is HTTP-cacheable, requires no schema coordination across teams, and solves the overfetching complaint at a fraction of the operational cost we paid for GraphQL.
key_technologies: REST API with sparse fieldsets (?fields= parameter)
Zod for schema validation and field filtering
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s, no HTTP caching, and schema maintenance burden proved too high; we already migrated back to REST in June 2026

reply.proposal: Bundle the partner widget as a Module Federation remote using Webpack, matching the existing dashboard's Module Federation setup. This reuses the proven runtime composition pattern we already run in production for embedded entry points, avoids introducing a second bundler into the dashboard build pipeline, and lets the host shell load the widget at runtime with shared dependency negotiation already solved. The widget gets its own entry point and can be deployed independently while consuming shared libraries (React, Zod schemas, pino) from the host.
acknowledged_rejections: Vite federation plugin — Already rejected in 2026-06-15 as experimental and unreliable for shared-dependency versioning; no change in status since then

  - REJECTED: staying on GraphQL — Resolver N+1s, no HTTP caching, and a schema only two people could safely change
- [2026-06-15] Bundle the dashboard with Webpack, using Module Federation for embedded entry points — The partner-facing shell requires Module Federation at runtime; the Vite federation plugin was not production-ready when we needed it
  - REJECTED: Vite federation plugin — Experimental; broke shared-dependency versioning in the spike
- [2026-05-18] Run all backend services on ECS Fargate — Two engineers cannot carry cluster upgrades, CNI debugging and node pools; Fargate covers every workload we actually run
  - REJECTED: self-managed control plane — Operational surface far beyond what this team can staff
- [2026-03-15] Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth

reply.proposal: Implement an in-memory session cache layer using Node.js LRU cache (lru-cache package) on each API instance, with cache invalidation via a lightweight pub/sub mechanism (Redis Pub/Sub or AWS SNS) when sessions are modified. This avoids a third datastore while keeping session reads local and fast; the Postgres UNLOGGED table remains the source of truth for durability and cross-instance consistency. For high-traffic endpoints, add a short TTL (30–60s) to the in-memory cache to bound staleness, and invalidate aggressively on logout or permission changes to prevent security issues.
key_technologies: Redis Pub/Sub (or AWS SNS for invalidation)
Node.js EventEmitter for local cache lifecycle
acknowledged_rejections: Adding a second cache layer (e.g., Redis) — We already rejected Redis to reduce ops burden; in-memory LRU on each instance achieves similar latency without a new datastore
Vite federation plugin for dashboard bundling — Not applicable to this session caching task, but noted as rejected in prior decisions

verdict.matchedOn: key_technologies: Redis Pub/Sub (or AWS SNS for invalidation)

- [2026-05-18] Run all backend services on ECS Fargate — Two engineers cannot carry cluster upgrades, CNI debugging and node pools; Fargate covers every workload we actually run
  - REJECTED: self-managed control plane — Operational surface far beyond what this team can staff

  - REJECTED: staying on GraphQL — Resolver N+1s, no HTTP caching, and a schema only two people could safely change
- [2026-06-05] Move the session cache from Redis to a Postgres UNLOGGED table — UNLOGGED benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our read pattern, and drops a second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure
  - REJECTED: staying on Redis — A second datastore's ops and on-call burden for a 15% read win we don't need
- [2026-06-15] Bundle the dashboard with Webpack, using Module Federation for embedded entry points — The partner-facing shell requires Module Federation at runtime; the Vite federation plugin was not production-ready when we needed it

- [2026-05-12] OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat

reply.proposal: Deploy the webhooks service on ECS Fargate, following our established pattern. Use CloudFormation or Terraform to define the task definition, service, and auto-scaling policies alongside our existing backend infrastructure-as-code. Wire CloudWatch Logs for observability and instrument with OpenTelemetry at 10% sampling to match our tracing setup. For webhook delivery reliability, add a DLQ-backed SQS queue for retries and a Postgres table (in our existing RDS instance) to track delivery state, avoiding a new Redis dependency.
acknowledged_rejections: Self-managed Kubernetes or EC2 cluster — operational burden beyond team capacity
Redis for webhook state or retry queues — we eliminated Redis as a second datastore in 2026-06-05
Custom retry logic without a queue — SQS provides durability and decoupling we'd otherwise have to build

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets on the REST API using the ?fields= query parameter, allowing clients to specify comma-separated field names per resource type. This leverages the existing REST migration decision and aligns with our move away from GraphQL's complexity; the mobile team gets precise field selection without resolver overhead, HTTP caching, or schema coordination friction. Clients can compose requests like GET /users/123?fields=id,name,email, and we document the available fields per endpoint in OpenAPI. This approach costs minimal backend work—a middleware layer to filter response objects—and gives mobile the overfetching relief they need without introducing a new query language or datastore.
key_technologies: REST API with sparse fieldsets (?fields= parameter)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — Already rejected 2026-06-20 due to resolver N+1s, cache invalidation complexity, and schema maintenance burden

reply.proposal: Bundle the partner widget as a Webpack Module Federation remote entry point, consistent with our existing dashboard architecture and the shell's runtime loading requirements. Use the same Webpack configuration and shared-dependency strategy we've already validated in production for the dashboard itself. This avoids introducing a new bundler into the pipeline and leverages the federation setup we've already debugged and tuned for embedded entry points.
key_technologies: ECS Fargate (for hosting the remote entry)
REST API with sparse fieldsets (for widget data)
acknowledged_rejections: Vite federation plugin — Experimental and broke shared-dependency versioning in our prior spike; not production-ready for our partner-facing requirements
